---
title: "Matching and Weighting Workshop"
subtitle: "Core concepts and lab launch for a 60-minute session"
author: "Luke Heley"
date: 7 March 2026
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [white, workshop-theme.scss]
    slide-level: 2
    slide-number: c/t
    progress: true
    controls: true
    overview: true
    transition: fade
    center: false
    width: 1400
    height: 850
    footer: "Matching and Weighting"
    preview-links: auto
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
---

## Session Promise

::: {.kicker}
This hour is about design judgement, not software mimicry.
:::

- explain what matching and weighting do before outcome analysis
- compare exact matching, `CEM`, and entropy balancing on the same `ATT`
- leave knowing which lab to open next and which sections to run first

## 60-Minute Arc

| Time | Segment | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `0-10` | Frame the problem | Why observational comparisons need design work |
| `10-20` | Build the workflow | Estimand, covariates, raw imbalance, overlap |
| `20-35` | Compare methods | Exact matching, `CEM`, entropy balancing |
| `35-45` | Revisit the main report | Turn the `lalonde` example into a decision rule |
| `45-60` | Launch the labs | Tell people exactly what to run and what to record |

## The Comparison Problem

::: {.lab-grid}
::: {.lab-card}
**Observational data**

- rarely give you a ready-made control group
- can hide major baseline differences behind one coefficient
- require design work before effect interpretation
:::

::: {.lab-card}
**Adjustment tools**

- `matching` restricts who compares to whom
- `weighting` changes how much each control counts
- neither method rescues weak covariates or no overlap
:::
:::

## The Non-Negotiable Workflow

::: {.card-grid}
::: {.mini-card}
**1. Name the target**

State the estimand before any adjustment output.
:::

::: {.mini-card}
**2. Choose covariates**

Keep only defensible pre-treatment variables in the design.
:::

::: {.mini-card}
**3. Inspect the raw sample**

Look at imbalance and overlap before touching a method.
:::

::: {.mini-card}
**4. Adjust the design**

Compare methods on the same target rather than moving the goalposts.
:::

::: {.mini-card}
**5. Estimate last**

Only interpret the effect once the design is credible enough to defend.
:::
:::

## Read Design Quality On Four Axes

::: {.metric-grid}
::: {.metric-card}
**Balance**

How much observed covariate imbalance remains?
:::

::: {.metric-card}
**Overlap**

Do treated and control units still occupy common support?
:::

::: {.metric-card}
**Retention**

How much of the treated sample survives the design?
:::

::: {.metric-card}
**Weight concentration**

If weighting is used, did the effective sample size collapse?
:::
:::

## Method Map

| Method | Best use | Main risk |
|---|---|---|
| Exact matching | Low-dimensional, highly auditable comparisons | Important continuous differences stay outside the exact rules |
| `CEM` | Inspectable matched-sample compromise across mixed covariates | Cutpoints prune treated units and change who the estimate describes |
| Entropy balancing | Preserve the treated group while forcing very strong observed balance | A few controls can carry too much weight |

## Worked Example {.section-slide}

The `lalonde` report is not a software tour.

It is a comparison of design costs on one shared `ATT`.

## Main Report Anchor

::: {.signal}
If you keep one page open during delivery, keep the main report open.
:::

- Data: `MatchIt::lalonde`
- Target: `ATT` for programme participants
- Key walkthrough order:
  - `Pre-Match Diagnostics`
  - `Exact Matching`
  - `Coarsened Exact Matching`
  - `Entropy Balancing for Weighting`
  - `Comparing the Methods`

[Open the main report](../articles/matching_methods_report.html)

## What The Main Report Teaches

::: {.card-grid}
::: {.mini-card}
**Exact matching**

- clearest audit trail
- retains `183 / 185` treated units
- still leaves one reported covariate above `0.1`
:::

::: {.mini-card}
**`CEM`**

- best matched-sample compromise
- worst adjusted abs. SMD about `0.077`
- retains only `81` treated units and `68` controls
:::

::: {.mini-card}
**Entropy balancing**

- preserves all `185` treated units
- drives included mean differences to about `0`
- control `ESS` falls to about `98.458`
:::
:::

## Decision Rule, Not Winner's Podium

- Choose the strongest design the data can defend.
- Prefer stronger quasi-experimental designs when assignment, timing, or cutoffs support them.
- Use exact matching for narrow low-dimensional auditability.
- Use `CEM` when you want an inspectable matched sample and can defend the coarsening rules.
- Use entropy balancing when preserving the treated population matters most and the weights stay credible.
- A defensible answer can still be: the available data do not support this comparison cleanly enough.

## Lab Launch {.section-slide}

The labs reuse the same workflow.

What changes is the data structure, the support problem, and the kind of design judgement you need to defend.

## What Stays Fixed Across Every Lab

::: {.lab-grid}
::: {.lab-card}
**Always do this**

- define treatment, outcome, covariates, and estimand first
- inspect the raw comparison before adjustment
- compare exact matching, `CEM`, and weighting on the same target where relevant
:::

::: {.lab-card}
**Always record this**

- worst pre/post imbalance
- treated retention or control `ESS`
- final estimate or benchmark gap
- one design choice and one limitation
:::
:::

## Lab Bundle At A Glance

| Lab | Best use in teaching | Core run order |
|---|---|---|
| Black Politicians | First post-talk matching exercise | `Step 1` to `Step 7` |
| NSW + CPS | Benchmark recovery against an experiment | `Step 1` to `Step 8` |
| HISP IE in Practice | When matching should not be the default | `Step 1` to `Step 10` |
| Titanic | Short support-cells and exact-matching recap | `Support Cells` to `Comparison Of Estimates` |

## What Every Group Should Hand Back

- the estimand they targeted
- the covariates they treated as design variables
- the worst raw imbalance and the best adjusted imbalance they achieved
- the retention or weighting cost they had to admit
- the design they would defend, plus one claim they still would not make

## Exercise 1: Black Politicians

::: {.lab-grid}
::: {.lab-card}
**Run these sections**

- `Step 1: Load The Data`
- `Step 2: Describe The Raw Comparison`
- `Step 3: Baseline Balance Diagnostics`
- `Step 4` to `Step 6`: exact, `CEM`, entropy balancing
- `Step 7: Estimate And Compare`
:::

::: {.lab-card}
**Capture these outputs**

- `raw_summary`
- pre-adjustment balance benchmark
- exact and `CEM` matched counts
- `ebal_weight_summary`
- the comparison table you fill in at the end
:::
:::

::: {.small-note}
Default first breakout: strongest direct continuation of the main report's logic.
:::

## Exercise 2: NSW + CPS Benchmark

::: {.lab-grid}
::: {.lab-card}
**Run these sections**

- `Step 1: Load Packages And Build Analysis Datasets`
- `Step 2: Compute The Experimental Benchmark`
- `Step 3: Raw Observational Estimate`
- `Step 4: Baseline Balance And Overlap Diagnostics`
- `Step 5` to `Step 7`: exact, `CEM`, entropy balancing
- `Step 8: Compare Against The Experimental Benchmark`
:::

::: {.lab-card}
**Capture these outputs**

- `benchmark_summary`
- `overlap_summary`
- exact and `CEM` matched counts
- `ebal_weight_summary`
- `estimate_comparison` with benchmark gaps
:::
:::

::: {.small-note}
Best benchmark question: which design gets closest to the experiment without hiding too much cost?
:::

## Exercise 3: HISP IE in Practice

::: {.lab-grid}
::: {.lab-card}
**Run this in phases**

- `Step 1`: convert the Stata files and load `evaluation.csv`
- `Step 2` and `Step 3`: naive contrasts to show weak designs
- `Step 4` to `Step 7`: randomized assignment, `IV`, `RDD`, and `DiD`
- `Step 8` and `Step 9`: matching and matched `DiD`
- `Step 10`: power calculations only if time remains
:::

::: {.lab-card}
**Capture these outputs**

- effect movement across the do-file sequence
- baseline-balance checks in the randomized branch
- matching-branch diagnostics
- one sentence on whether matching adds anything once stronger designs are available
:::
:::

::: {.small-note}
Use this lab to teach escalation: matching is part of the toolkit, not the automatic endpoint.
:::

## Exercise 4: Titanic

::: {.lab-grid}
::: {.lab-card}
**Run these sections**

- `Support Cells Before Matching`
- `Exact Matching`
- `Propensity-Score Subclassification`
- `Comparison Of Estimates`
:::

::: {.lab-card}
**Capture these outputs**

- the support-cell table
- the balance-comparison table
- subclass counts
- the final estimate table
:::
:::

::: {.small-note}
Shortest follow-on exercise when you want the support logic to stay visible on one page.
:::

## Handoff

::: {.signal}
Open the main report, the workshop notes, and the first lab before you start the practical section.
:::

- [Workshop speaking notes](matching_workshop_notes.html)
- [Main report](../articles/matching_methods_report.html)
- [Labs hub](../labs/index.html)
- [Black Politicians lab](../labs/black_politicians_lab.html)

## Closing Line

::: {.kicker}
Use the strongest design the data can defend, then report the cost of that design plainly.
:::
